title: Cat Chewing on One Side: Causes and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat chewing on one side? Learn about dental pain in cats, why it's a medium-urgency issue, and how photos help your vet triage the problem.
content_html:
TL;DR: One-sided chewing in cats is a sign of dental pain or oral injury that requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. Seek immediate care if your cat stops eating entirely or develops facial swelling.

What does it mean if my cat is only chewing on one side of their mouth?

When a cat exclusively chews food on one side of their mouth, it is a significant clinical sign of oral discomfort. This behavior, known as unilateral mastication, occurs when a cat tries to avoid putting pressure on a painful tooth, inflamed gums, or an oral lesion. Because cats are evolutionarily programmed to hide pain, this subtle change in eating habits is often one of the few outward signs that something is wrong inside their mouth.

Is it an emergency if my cat starts chewing on one side?

  • The urgency level for a cat chewing on one side is Medium, indicating active pain that should not be ignored.
  • Schedule a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if the behavior is accompanied by a total refusal to eat, facial swelling, or extreme lethargy to prevent dehydration and hepatic lipidosis.

How can a photo of my cat’s mouth help with veterinary triage?

  • If your cat is comfortable, gently lift their lip to take a clear, well-lit photo of the teeth and gums.
  • A photo allows a veterinary professional to identify localized redness, broken crowns, or growths without the stress of a physical struggle.
  • This visual aid helps the triage team determine if the issue is a fractured tooth requiring surgery or a soft tissue infection requiring antibiotics, allowing them to prioritize your cat's appointment appropriately.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Unilateral chewing in cats can be indicative of oral pain due to dental disease, oral foreign bodies, neoplasms, injuries, or mucosal defects. Other causes include infectious diseases, reluctance to swallow due to esophageal or gastrointestinal irritation, sublingual lesions, tonsillitis, administration of medication, conformational defects, metabolic disorders such as hepatic encephalopathy or uremia, and salivary gland issues. A thorough oral examination under anesthesia, along with radiographs, is crucial to identify the underlying cause. The possibility of rabies should be eliminated before oral examination.

Chapter: Dentistry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2008)

faqs.question: Could my cat just have a preference for one side?
faqs.answer: Unlike humans, cats do not typically have a 'preferred' side for chewing. If they suddenly switch to one side, it is almost always due to pain or a physical obstruction on the opposite side.

faqs.question: What are common causes for this behavior?
faqs.answer: Common causes include tooth resorption (FORLs), fractured teeth, periodontal disease, gingivostomatitis, or oral tumors.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relievers?
faqs.answer: No. Never give a cat human pain medication like Tylenol or Ibuprofen, as these are highly toxic and can be fatal to felines. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.
